microsoft_native.name: Microsoft-Native SOC
microsoft_native.tagline: Defender XDR + Sentinel — turn the licensing you already pay for into a real security operation.
microsoft_native.description: You're already in the Microsoft ecosystem, so the fastest route to visibility is upgrading licensing (Business Premium or E5) and switching on what's included: Defender for Endpoint, Defender for Office 365, Entra ID Protection, and Microsoft Sentinel as the SIEM. One vendor, one portal, incidents pre-correlated across identity, endpoint, and email. The trade-offs are vendor lock-in and Sentinel ingest costs that need active watching.

open_source.name: Open-Source / Lean SOC
open_source.tagline: Wazuh, Security Onion, and free CISA services — maximum visibility for the price of your time.
open_source.description: Every monitoring domain gets covered with proven open-source tooling: Wazuh as the SIEM/XDR core, Security Onion for network monitoring, Greenbone for vulnerability scanning, TheHive for case management. The dollar cost is small; the real cost is engineering time to deploy, tune, and maintain it — budget roughly a day a week of care and feeding, and be honest with leadership about that trade.

best_of_breed.name: Best-of-Breed SOC
best_of_breed.tagline: CrowdStrike, Splunk, Tenable, Proofpoint — the classic funded stack, each layer best-in-class.
best_of_breed.description: Pick the market leader in each layer and integrate them: CrowdStrike on endpoints, Splunk on logs, Tenable on vulnerabilities, Proofpoint on email, Okta on identity. This buys the best detection quality and vendor support available. The trade-offs are cost, managing contracts across six vendors, and integration work that single-vendor shops get for free.

BUILD_STEPS.title: Asset & Identity Inventory
BUILD_STEPS.what: A complete, living list of everything you're defending: devices (laptops, servers, VMs, cloud workloads), identities (users, admins, service accounts), SaaS applications, and internet-facing assets. This is the first thing a SOC engineer builds in a new company — not a tool, a list.
BUILD_STEPS.why: You cannot monitor or secure what you don't know exists. Every later step is measured against this inventory: EDR coverage %, log sources onboarded, patch scope. Attackers specialize in finding the forgotten server and the stale admin account — your inventory is how you find them first.
day_one: Ask for any existing inventory: RMM, Intune/MDM, AD, spreadsheets — then assume all of them are incomplete.
Export every user account with last-logon date; flag anything unused for 90+ days.
Count the domain/global admins. If the number surprises people, write it down — that's finding #1.
Pull DNS zones and public IP allocations; you'll validate what's actually exposed in Step 2.
Ask finance for SaaS invoices and OAuth app grants — shadow IT lives in the expense reports.

products.name: Microsoft Intune + Entra ID
products.note: Enroll every device; Entra gives you users, groups, sign-in activity, and registered apps in one place.

products.name: Azure Resource Graph
products.note: One KQL query enumerates every subscription, VM, and public IP across the tenant.

products.name: Defender for Endpoint device discovery
products.note: Onboarded endpoints passively spot unmanaged neighbors on the same network.

alternatives.name: Axonius / runZero
alternatives.comparison: Purpose-built asset aggregation with far better reconciliation across sources — worth it later, overkill for day 30.

microsoft_native.ops_notes: Intune enrollment is the long pole — expect stragglers and BYOD exceptions. Review the Entra 'stale devices' and 'inactive users' reports monthly and make cleanup someone's named job, or the inventory rots within a quarter.
config_steps: Enforce Intune enrollment via conditional access: unenrolled devices lose access to company data.
Run an Azure Resource Graph query for all resources with public IPs; export to your asset register.
Enable device discovery in the Defender portal and reconcile 'unmanaged devices' weekly.
Export Entra users with lastSignInDateTime; disable accounts inactive 90+ days (after owner sign-off).
Review Entra 'Enterprise Applications' for OAuth grants — revoke anything unrecognized.

products.name: NetBox
products.note: The de-facto open-source source of truth for networks, devices, and IP space.

products.name: Nmap + scheduled discovery scans
products.note: Weekly ping/TCP sweeps of every internal subnet, diffed against NetBox.

products.name: osquery (via Fleet)
products.note: SQL queries against live endpoints: installed software, users, listening ports.

alternatives.name: Spreadsheet + AD export
alternatives.comparison: Fine for week 1, but it's stale the day you finish it — automate discovery or it's fiction.

alternatives.name: runZero Community
alternatives.comparison: Excellent unauthenticated discovery with a free tier for small networks; less flexible than NetBox as a system of record.

open_source.ops_notes: NetBox and Fleet are two more servers you now own — patch them like production, because they are. Put the discovery scan on cron and alert on *new* assets appearing, not just on the scan completing.
config_steps: Stand up NetBox; load subnets, VLANs, and known devices as the baseline.
Schedule weekly Nmap discovery of all internal ranges; diff results against NetBox and alert on new hosts.
Deploy osquery/Fleet to all servers first, then workstations, for software and account visibility.
Export directory accounts (AD or Google Workspace) with last-login; disable 90-day-stale accounts.
Record every SaaS app with an owner and an auth method (SSO vs. local password) — this list drives Step 3.

products.name: runZero Enterprise
products.note: Best-in-class unauthenticated discovery; finds the printers, IoT, and forgotten subnets agents miss.

products.name: Axonius
products.note: Correlates EDR, MDM, directory, and cloud APIs into one deduplicated inventory with coverage gaps highlighted.

products.name: Cloud-native inventories (AWS Config / Azure Resource Graph)
products.note: Feed cloud resource data into Axonius rather than tracking it by hand.

alternatives.name: ServiceNow CMDB
alternatives.comparison: The enterprise answer if ITSM already runs on ServiceNow — powerful, but a project measured in quarters, not weeks.

best_of_breed.ops_notes: CAASM tools are only as good as the sources you connect — wire in EDR, MDM, IdP, and cloud APIs in week one. The killer report is 'devices seen on the network with no EDR agent'; schedule it weekly to your inbox.
config_steps: Deploy runZero explorers on each site/VLAN; run full discovery across all internal ranges.
Connect Axonius adapters: EDR, MDM, identity provider, cloud accounts, vulnerability scanner.
Build the two golden queries: assets with no EDR agent, and assets in no patch group.
Export all identities with last-login and privilege level; disable stale accounts with owner sign-off.
Tag crown-jewel systems (from Step 2) in the inventory so every later alert can be prioritized by asset value.

BUILD_STEPS.title: Crown Jewels & Attack Surface
BUILD_STEPS.what: Two short lists that drive every priority call for the next year: (1) crown jewels — the systems and data the business cannot afford to lose, and (2) the external attack surface — everything reachable from the internet. Plus a lightweight threat model: who would attack this company and how.
BUILD_STEPS.why: A 90-day plan fails if you try to protect everything equally. Ransomware crews scan the entire internet for one exposed service; meanwhile the finance database matters more than the lobby signage PC. Knowing what's exposed and what's critical is how a one-person SOC punches above its weight.
day_one: Ask leadership one question: 'What would actually stop the business tomorrow?' Write down the top 5 answers.
Look up your public IPs and domains in Shodan/Censys before an attacker does.
Check for exposed RDP (3389), SMB (445), and admin panels on anything internet-facing — these get exploited within hours, not weeks.
Find expired or soon-to-expire TLS certificates — they're a proxy for unmanaged systems.
Ask if there's ever been a pentest or audit; the last report is a free roadmap of known weaknesses.

products.name: Microsoft Defender EASM
products.note: Continuously discovers and monitors internet-facing assets tied to your domains and IPs.

products.name: Microsoft Secure Score
products.note: Imperfect but great for exec reporting — a number that goes up as you complete this plan.

alternatives.name: Shodan Monitor
alternatives.comparison: ~$69/mo watches your IP ranges for new exposures — cheaper, less integrated than EASM.

microsoft_native.ops_notes: EASM seeds matter: give it every domain, registrar account, and IP block or it maps someone else's assets. Review new findings weekly for the first month, then monthly. Secure Score improvements should be logged as change tickets — some recommendations break legacy workflows.
config_steps: Seed Defender EASM with all domains, subdomains, and public IP ranges from Step 1.
Triage the first EASM report: kill exposed RDP/SMB immediately, ticket everything else by severity.
Baseline Secure Score and set a 90-day target with your top 10 improvement actions.
Interview stakeholders and document the top 5 crown-jewel systems, their owners, and where they live.
Write a one-page threat model: likely attackers (commodity ransomware, BEC, insider), likely paths in.

products.name: OWASP Amass + Nmap
products.note: Enumerate your subdomains and scan your own public ranges exactly like an attacker would.

products.name: Shodan (small business tier)
products.note: One of the few paid line items worth it on a lean budget — alerts when something new appears on your IPs.

products.name: CISA Cyber Hygiene service
products.note: Weekly external vulnerability scans of your public IPs, run by CISA, with a report emailed to you.

alternatives.name: testssl.sh + crt.sh
alternatives.comparison: Free certificate-transparency and TLS auditing to catch forgotten subdomains and weak configs.

open_source.ops_notes: CISA Cyber Hygiene enrollment takes a couple of weeks — sign up on day one so scans are running by week three. Re-run Amass monthly; new subdomains appearing that you didn't create is a finding.
config_steps: Run Amass against every company domain; reconcile discovered subdomains against DNS zone exports.
Nmap your own public IP ranges from an outside connection; document every open port and its owner.
Enroll in CISA Cyber Hygiene (vulnerability scanning) — free continuous external scanning.
Set up Shodan Monitor on your IP ranges with email alerts for new services.
Document crown jewels + a one-page threat model; get leadership to sign it so priorities are agreed, not assumed.

products.name: Palo Alto Cortex Xpanse (or CyCognito)
products.note: Attacker's-eye view of your perimeter with continuous discovery and prioritized exposures.

products.name: Pentest / red-team engagement
products.note: A scoped external + phishing engagement in the first 6 months validates the whole program against reality.

alternatives.name: Tenable Attack Surface Management
alternatives.comparison: Cheaper when bundled with the Tenable VM stack you're buying in Step 7 — one vendor, one pane.

best_of_breed.ops_notes: EASM platforms surface findings faster than most teams can fix them — agree SLAs up front (internet-facing critical: 48 hours). Schedule the pentest for month 4–6, after the basics land, so you're paying to find real gaps rather than documenting known ones.
config_steps: Onboard all domains and IP space into the EASM platform; validate the discovered asset list against Step 1.
Fix or ticket every critical exposure (exposed management interfaces, EOL services) within the first two weeks.
Feed EASM findings into the vulnerability SLA process you'll formalize in Step 7.
Document crown-jewel systems with owners, data classification, and recovery priority.
Book a pentest for month 4–6 with success criteria tied to your new detections (did the SOC see it?).

BUILD_STEPS.title: Identity Security & MFA
BUILD_STEPS.what: Locking down how people prove who they are: MFA enforced everywhere (especially email, VPN, and admin accounts), legacy authentication protocols disabled, privileged accounts inventoried and minimized, and baseline conditional access / SSO policies.
BUILD_STEPS.why: Stolen credentials are the #1 initial access vector — most 'hacks' are just logins. Identity is the control that pays off fastest: enforcing MFA and killing legacy auth blocks the majority of commodity account-takeover attacks in a single change window.
day_one: Measure real MFA coverage — % of users registered AND % of sign-ins actually challenged. The gap is your exposure.
Hunt for legacy authentication (IMAP/POP/SMTP basic auth) in sign-in logs — it bypasses MFA entirely.
List every privileged account (global/domain admin). Target: fewer than 5 humans, zero shared accounts.
Check for a break-glass account: one emergency admin, long random password, excluded from MFA policy, alarmed on use.
Find service accounts with human-style passwords that never rotate — note them for later, don't break them yet.

products.name: Entra ID P1 (in Business Premium)
products.note: Conditional access is the enforcement engine: require MFA, block legacy auth, restrict by device compliance.

products.name: Entra ID Protection (P2 / E5)
products.note: Flags leaked credentials, impossible travel, and anomalous sign-ins; can auto-force password reset.

products.name: Entra PIM (P2 / E5)
products.note: Admins elevate just-in-time with approval and audit trail instead of holding standing global admin.

alternatives.name: Security defaults
alternatives.comparison: Free tenant-wide MFA + legacy auth block. No granularity, but infinitely better than nothing while licensing is sorted.

microsoft_native.ops_notes: Roll out in report-only mode first, review the sign-in log impact, then enforce — a bad conditional access policy locks out the CEO at the airport. Keep one documented break-glass account excluded from CA, and alert on any use of it.
config_steps: Create the break-glass account first; store credentials offline, alert on any sign-in.
Deploy CA policies in report-only: require MFA for all users, block legacy auth, require compliant device for admins.
Review report-only impact for a week, fix stragglers (conference room accounts, scanners), then enforce.
Migrate all admins into PIM with just-in-time elevation; reduce standing global admins to the break-glass only.
Enable Identity Protection policies: high-risk sign-in → MFA challenge; high-risk user → password reset.

products.name: Built-in IdP MFA (Google Workspace / Entra free tier)
products.note: Your existing suite includes MFA enforcement — turning it on costs nothing but a comms plan.

products.name: Duo Essentials
products.note: The pragmatic paid exception on a lean budget: MFA on VPN, servers, and anything SAML/RADIUS.

products.name: Keycloak
products.note: Open-source SSO/IdP to centralize auth for internal apps instead of local passwords everywhere.

alternatives.name: Hardware keys (FIDO2) for admins
alternatives.comparison: ~$30/key one-time; phishing-resistant MFA for the accounts that matter most. Cheap, huge upgrade over push approvals.

open_source.ops_notes: Enforce MFA in waves — admins day one, IT week one, everyone by week four — with loud comms and a helpdesk script for lockouts. Legacy auth will break an old scanner or a billing integration somewhere; find it in the logs first, fix the integration, then block.
config_steps: Enforce MFA for all admin accounts today; issue FIDO2 keys to admins if budget allows.
Enable MFA enforcement for all users with a 2–4 week enrollment window and clear comms.
Disable legacy auth protocols (IMAP/POP/SMTP basic) after confirming in logs nothing legitimate uses them.
Front the VPN and server logins with Duo (RADIUS/PAM integration).
Audit privileged accounts: separate admin accounts from daily-driver accounts, kill shared logins, document a break-glass.

products.name: Okta Workforce Identity
products.note: Centralize every SaaS app behind one IdP with adaptive policies and clean offboarding.

products.name: YubiKey 5 series (FIDO2)
products.note: Hardware keys for admins, finance, and executives — immune to push fatigue and AiTM phishing kits.

products.name: 1Password Business (or CyberArk later)
products.note: Shared-secret hygiene now; full PAM (CyberArk/Delinea) is a year-two project.

alternatives.name: Duo Advantage
alternatives.comparison: ~$6 user/mo; stronger device-trust story, weaker as a full IdP than Okta. Fine if the IdP is staying Microsoft/Google.

best_of_breed.ops_notes: The SSO migration is the project here — inventory every app's auth method in Step 1, then migrate the top 20 by risk. Every app behind Okta means offboarding is one click; every app outside it is a future orphaned account.
config_steps: Stand up Okta, federate the directory, and migrate email + the top 10 SaaS apps behind SSO.
Enforce adaptive MFA globally; require phishing-resistant factors (FIDO2) for admin roles.
Disable legacy authentication at the email platform after a log review for legitimate use.
Move all shared credentials into the vault; rotate anything that lived in a spreadsheet or wiki.
Set the offboarding runbook: HR trigger → Okta deactivation → session revocation → device wipe, same day.

BUILD_STEPS.title: Endpoint Detection & Response (EDR)
BUILD_STEPS.what: An EDR agent on every workstation and server — not legacy antivirus. EDR records process, network, and file telemetry, detects behavior (not just signatures), and lets you remotely isolate a machine or kill a process from the console.
BUILD_STEPS.why: The endpoint is where the phishing attachment detonates and where ransomware runs. Without EDR you find out about compromises from the ransom note; with it you get the alert at the first suspicious process and can cut a machine off the network in seconds from anywhere.
day_one: Find out what's on endpoints today (built-in Defender? An expired McAfee from 2019?) and whether anyone reads its console.
Compute coverage: agents reporting ÷ devices in your Step-1 inventory. The gap is where the incident will start.
Check servers specifically — they're routinely forgotten, and they're the highest-value targets.
Verify tamper protection is on; commodity malware disables unprotected AV as its first move.
Check how many users are local admins on their machines — that number is a roadmap item.

products.name: Defender for Business (in M365 Business Premium)
products.note: Real EDR (behavioral detection, isolation, auto-investigation) for orgs up to 300 seats.

products.name: Defender for Endpoint P2 (E5)
products.note: Adds advanced hunting (KQL over raw telemetry), threat analytics, and 30-day searchable timeline.

products.name: Defender for Servers (via Defender for Cloud)
products.note: Same agent and portal for Windows/Linux servers, on-prem or cloud, via Azure Arc.

alternatives.name: CrowdStrike / SentinelOne on top of M365
alternatives.comparison: Better raw EDR in most bake-offs, but you lose the free XDR correlation with identity and email signals — and pay twice.

microsoft_native.ops_notes: Onboard via Intune policy so new machines get the agent automatically — manual onboarding guarantees drift. Turn on tamper protection tenant-wide and set automated investigation to 'full remediation' once you trust it (start in audit for week one).
config_steps: Onboard all workstations to Defender via Intune; verify each device reports healthy in the portal.
Arc-enroll and onboard servers with Defender for Servers P2 — servers first if you must stage.
Enable tamper protection, cloud-delivered protection, and EDR in block mode tenant-wide.
Enable attack surface reduction (ASR) rules in audit, review 2 weeks, then enforce the low-noise set.
Run an isolation drill: isolate a test machine from the portal, confirm it drops network, release it, write down the steps.

products.name: Wazuh agent (+ Sysmon on Windows)
products.note: FIM, vulnerability detection, log collection, and active response. Sysmon supplies the deep process telemetry.

products.name: Microsoft Defender AV (built-in)
products.note: Don't rip out the built-in engine — Wazuh watches it and collects its alerts centrally.

products.name: LimaCharlie
products.note: If pure open-source response is too thin, this is near-EDR capability at open-source-adjacent pricing.

alternatives.name: Velociraptor
alternatives.comparison: Superb free DFIR/hunting tool — pair with Wazuh for investigations; it's a scalpel, not a monitoring platform.

open_source.ops_notes: Be honest about the gap: Wazuh + Sysmon gives strong detection visibility but weaker one-click response than commercial EDR — your 'isolate host' is a firewall rule or a walk to the desk. Deploy Sysmon with the SwiftOnSecurity config as the starting point and version-control it.
config_steps: Deploy Sysmon (SwiftOnSecurity config) to all Windows endpoints via GPO/script.
Deploy the Wazuh agent to servers first, then workstations; confirm every Step-1 asset reports in.
Enable Wazuh modules: FIM on critical paths, vulnerability detection, and Windows Defender log collection.
Configure Wazuh active response for the obvious cases (block IP after repeated auth failures).
Write the manual isolation runbook: who pulls the VLAN/switch port or firewall-blocks a host, and how fast.

products.name: CrowdStrike Falcon (Pro/Enterprise)
products.note: Consistent leader in detection quality; lightweight agent; one-click network containment from the console.

products.name: Falcon Complete MDR
products.note: If nobody is watching the console at 2am, pay for the team that is — MDR beats an unwatched tool.

alternatives.name: SentinelOne Singularity
alternatives.comparison: Comparable detection with stronger autonomous rollback (ransomware undo); often meaningfully cheaper at renewal.

alternatives.name: Sophos Intercept X + MDR
alternatives.comparison: The budget-conscious MDR bundle; less telemetry depth than CrowdStrike/S1 but a strong managed service.

best_of_breed.ops_notes: Negotiate at year end and multi-year — EDR list prices are fiction. Turn on prevention mode after a 1–2 week detect-only bake; enable USB device control and firewall management modules only after the base rollout is stable.
config_steps: Deploy the Falcon sensor via MDM/GPO to all workstations and servers; reconcile against inventory weekly.
Run detect-only for 1–2 weeks, tune exclusions with vendors' guidance, then enable full prevention.
Enable network containment permissions for the SOC role; run an isolation drill and time it.
Integrate Falcon alerts into the SIEM (Step 5) and case management (Step 9).
If no 24/7 coverage exists in-house, scope MDR now — an unwatched EDR console is a false sense of security.

BUILD_STEPS.title: Centralized Logging & SIEM
BUILD_STEPS.what: One searchable place where the logs that matter land, with alerting on top: identity/auth events, endpoint detections, email verdicts, firewall/VPN, DNS, and cloud control-plane audit logs. Target 90 days hot searchability and ~1 year archive.
BUILD_STEPS.why: Correlation and investigation are impossible when evidence lives in six consoles with 7-day retention. When the incident comes, the first question is 'what happened before this alert?' — the SIEM is the only thing that can answer it. A SOC without centralized logs is a helpdesk with opinions.
day_one: Map what logs exist today, where they go, and how long they live. The usual answer — 'the firewall logs to itself for 7 days' — is finding #2.
Confirm the audit log is actually enabled in your productivity suite (M365 unified audit log / Google admin audit). It's not always on by default.
Check clock sync (NTP) and timezone consistency across systems — mismatched timestamps ruin investigations.
Rank log sources by value: identity sign-ins, EDR, email, firewall/VPN, DNS, cloud audit — onboard in that order.
Estimate daily log volume (GB/day) before signing anything — ingest pricing is where SIEM budgets die.

products.name: Microsoft Sentinel
products.note: Native connectors for the whole Microsoft estate; many M365 log types ingest free with E5 (up to a daily grant).

products.name: Log Analytics + archive tier
products.note: Keep 90 days interactive, archive to 12 months — most of your retention cost disappears.

alternatives.name: Defender XDR portal alone
alternatives.comparison: Free correlation across Defender products with 30-day retention — a legitimate starting point before Sentinel if budget gates you.

microsoft_native.ops_notes: Watch ingest like a utility bill: set a daily cap and cost alerts in week one, because one misconfigured verbose source can double the bill. Route chatty low-value sources (raw firewall allows) to basic/auxiliary tier tables rather than analytics tier.
config_steps: Deploy Sentinel on a new Log Analytics workspace; set daily cap and cost alert immediately.
Connect in order: Entra ID sign-in/audit, Defender XDR, Office 365 activity, then firewall syslog via the forwarder.
Confirm the M365 unified audit log is enabled and flowing.
Set retention: 90 days interactive, 12 months archive; put verbose network logs in a cheaper tier table.
Enable the vendor-recommended analytics rules for connected sources — then tune in Step 9, don't enable everything.

products.name: Wazuh (server + indexer + dashboard)
products.note: One platform does agent telemetry, log collection, rules, and dashboards — the lean-SOC workhorse.

products.name: rsyslog / syslog-ng front end
products.note: Firewalls, switches, and appliances syslog into a collector that feeds Wazuh.

alternatives.name: Elastic Security (self-managed)
alternatives.comparison: More powerful search and a bigger detection ecosystem; heavier care-and-feeding than Wazuh for a small team.

alternatives.name: Graylog Open
alternatives.comparison: Excellent log management with alerting; fewer built-in security detections than Wazuh — better as pure log tier.

open_source.ops_notes: Size the indexer for 90 days hot and snapshot to cheap object storage for the year archive. The Wazuh server is now your most security-critical asset: MFA on the dashboard, patch monthly, and back up the config — an attacker who owns the SIEM owns the investigation.
config_steps: Deploy the Wazuh stack (single-node is fine to start) on a hardened VM; MFA the dashboard.
Point all agents (Step 4) at it; add syslog inputs for firewall, VPN, switches, and DNS.
Connect cloud/SaaS sources: M365 or Google Workspace audit via their Wazuh modules/APIs.
Set index lifecycle: 90 days hot, snapshot archive to object storage for 12 months.
Verify end-to-end: generate a test event (failed admin logins) and watch it arrive, alert, and notify.

products.name: Splunk Cloud
products.note: The industry-standard SIEM with the deepest content and talent ecosystem; priced by ingest or workload.

products.name: Splunk Enterprise Security
products.note: Correlation searches, risk-based alerting, and ES frameworks out of the box.

alternatives.name: Panther
alternatives.comparison: Detections-as-code on a cloud data lake — dramatically cheaper at high volume, best if your team is comfortable writing Python detections.

alternatives.name: Elastic Cloud Security
alternatives.comparison: Strong middle path on cost with good detections; less turnkey SOC content than Splunk ES.

best_of_breed.ops_notes: Ingest pricing punishes success — negotiate volume tiers up front and route low-value verbose logs to cheap storage (S3/data lake) with search-on-demand rather than full indexing. Assign a named detection engineer; Splunk without content ownership becomes an expensive archive.
config_steps: Stand up Splunk Cloud; onboard identity, EDR, email, firewall, DNS, and cloud audit logs in that order.
Deploy Enterprise Security and enable the correlation searches matching your top threats from Step 2.
Set retention 90 days searchable / 12 months archived; route verbose sources to low-cost storage.
Wire alert actions into the case/ticketing flow you'll formalize in Step 9.
Validate with an end-to-end test: simulated credential-stuffing burst → alert → ticket → analyst.

BUILD_STEPS.title: Email Security & Phishing Defense
BUILD_STEPS.what: Hardening the front door: DMARC/DKIM/SPF enforced so your domain can't be spoofed, advanced filtering for phishing and malware beyond the default spam filter, a one-click 'report phish' button for users, and a training program with realistic simulations.
BUILD_STEPS.why: Phishing remains the #1 initial access vector, and business email compromise quietly out-loses ransomware most years. Email is also where you have the most leverage: DNS records that cost nothing, filtering you may already license, and users who — given a report button — become sensors.
day_one: Check your DMARC record right now: none or p=none means anyone can spoof invoices from your domain today.
Audit mailbox forwarding rules and OAuth grants org-wide — auto-forward-to-Gmail is the classic BEC persistence you inherit, not create.
Look at the current filter's quarantine and who (if anyone) reviews it.
Ask how users report suspicious email today; 'they forward it to IT sometimes' means you have no detection from your best sensor network.
Check finance's payment-change process — a phone-verification policy defeats most BEC without any software.

products.name: Defender for Office 365 (P1 in Business Premium, P2 in E5)
products.note: Safe Links, Safe Attachments, impersonation protection, and (P2) automated investigation of reported mail.

products.name: Attack Simulation Training (P2/E5)
products.note: Realistic simulations with auto-assigned training for clickers — no third-party needed.

alternatives.name: KnowBe4 on top
alternatives.comparison: ~$10–30 user/yr; richer training content and reporting than the built-in simulator if culture-building is a priority.

microsoft_native.ops_notes: Move DMARC from p=none to quarantine to reject over ~60 days while watching aggregate reports — jumping straight to reject breaks the marketing platform someone forgot to mention. Enable the built-in Report button and actually triage what users submit; response time trains reporting behavior.
config_steps: Apply the Standard (then Strict) preset security policies in Defender for Office 365.
Publish SPF, enable DKIM signing on all sending domains, then set DMARC p=none with rua reporting.
Review DMARC aggregate reports for 2–4 weeks, authorize legitimate senders, then move to quarantine → reject.
Deploy the Report Phishing button via Outlook policy; route reports into the Defender submission queue.
Block auto-forwarding to external domains by policy; alert on new forwarding rules via Sentinel.

products.name: Google Workspace / M365 built-in protections
products.note: The default filters are decent when actually configured: enable enhanced pre-delivery scanning, external banners, and attachment sandboxing where offered.

products.name: dmarcian (free tier) or postmark DMARC digests
products.note: Turns raw DMARC XML into readable weekly reports so you can reach p=reject safely.

products.name: GoPhish
products.note: Run quarterly simulations yourself; pair with free training content (SANS OUCH!, CISA resources).

alternatives.name: Sublime Security (community)
alternatives.comparison: Free-tier detection-as-code email security for smaller orgs — a serious capability upgrade if you outgrow native filters.

open_source.ops_notes: The highest-ROI items here are free: DMARC at reject, external-sender banners, blocking auto-forward, and a finance callback policy for payment changes. Run GoPhish gently — the goal is reporting rate going up, not shaming clickers.
config_steps: Enable every advanced protection toggle your suite includes (enhanced scanning, suspicious attachment quarantine, external banners).
Publish SPF/DKIM/DMARC; monitor via dmarcian and walk DMARC to p=reject over 60 days.
Enable the native 'report phishing' mechanism and document who triages reports within what SLA.
Block external auto-forwarding; audit and remove existing forwarding rules and stale OAuth grants.
Get finance to adopt out-of-band verification for any payment or banking change — write it into policy.

products.name: Proofpoint (or Abnormal Security)
products.note: Proofpoint for classic gateway depth and threat intel; Abnormal for API-based behavioral BEC detection with near-zero tuning.

products.name: KnowBe4
products.note: The standard training + simulation platform; its reporting is board-meeting-ready.

alternatives.name: Material Security
alternatives.comparison: Protects the mailbox contents themselves (post-compromise) — pairs well with, rather than replaces, a gateway.

best_of_breed.ops_notes: API-based tools (Abnormal) deploy in an afternoon with no MX change and can run alongside the incumbent during evaluation — bake off before committing. Whoever you pick, the DMARC-to-reject project is still yours to drive; no vendor does it for you.
config_steps: Run a 30-day bake-off (API-based tools make this cheap) measured on missed-phish and false-positive counts.
Deploy the winner; integrate its verdict and quarantine events into the SIEM.
Publish SPF/DKIM/DMARC and drive to p=reject over 60 days with aggregate-report review.
Roll out KnowBe4: baseline simulation first, quarterly campaigns after, training auto-assigned to clickers.
Deploy the report-phish button and commit to a triage SLA; report the reporting rate monthly.

BUILD_STEPS.title: Vulnerability Management & Patching
BUILD_STEPS.what: A continuous cycle: scan everything for known vulnerabilities, prioritize by real-world exploitability (CISA KEV list and EPSS, not raw CVSS), and patch on defined SLAs — with automated patching for endpoints and a monthly cadence for servers and network gear.
BUILD_STEPS.why: Exploited edge devices — VPNs, firewalls, file-transfer appliances — now rival phishing as the way ransomware gets in, and those attacks need no user mistake at all. A scanner without patch SLAs is a report generator; the SLA is what turns findings into work.
day_one: Check firmware on every internet-facing device (VPN, firewall) against the vendor's latest — these are the CVEs being mass-exploited this week.
Cross-reference your external services against the CISA KEV catalog; anything on both lists is an emergency, not a ticket.
Assess Windows/macOS update health: are endpoints actually applying patches, or just downloading them?
Inventory EOL software and operating systems still in production — each one needs an owner and an exit date.
Find the last vulnerability scan report, if one exists; its age tells you the program's real maturity.

products.name: Defender Vulnerability Management
products.note: Agent-based, continuous, no scan windows — findings appear beside EDR data with exploit context.

products.name: Intune update rings + Windows Autopatch
products.note: Ringed OS and Office updates with automatic rollout pause on bad patches.

alternatives.name: Tenable alongside
alternatives.comparison: Adds unauthenticated network scanning for the gear the agent can't cover (appliances, printers, network devices) — a common hybrid.

microsoft_native.ops_notes: Agent-based VM only sees what runs an agent — your firewall and VPN firmware still need a manual monthly check against vendor advisories (or a network scanner). Publish the SLAs where everyone sees them: KEV/critical 7 days, high 30, medium 90.
config_steps: Enable Defender Vulnerability Management; triage the first report by KEV/exploit-available first, CVSS second.
Configure Intune update rings (pilot → broad → critical-systems) with enforced deadlines.
Create the manual tracker for network/appliance firmware with a monthly advisory-review cadence.
Publish patch SLAs and report compliance against them monthly — % of KEV vulns closed within 7 days.
Feed critical findings into the same ticket queue as incidents so they compete for attention honestly.

products.name: Greenbone Community Edition (OpenVAS)
products.note: Credentialed internal scanning of servers, endpoints, and network gear on a weekly schedule.

products.name: CISA KEV + EPSS feeds
products.note: Sort findings by 'actually being exploited' rather than drowning in CVSS 7+ noise.

products.name: OS-native tooling (Windows Update policies / GPO, unattended-upgrades)
products.note: Enforced auto-update policies close most endpoint findings without a patch product.

alternatives.name: Nuclei
alternatives.comparison: Free template-driven scanning that shines on web apps and exposed services — complement, not replacement, for Greenbone.

open_source.ops_notes: Credentialed scans or it barely counts — set up a dedicated scan account with least privilege and vault its password. Expect the first full scan to be overwhelming: triage only KEV + internet-facing first, and make peace with the long tail on a monthly burn-down.
config_steps: Deploy Greenbone on a dedicated VM; run a discovery scan, then credentialed scans of servers first.
Build the triage view: KEV-listed and internet-facing findings first; everything else monthly burn-down.
Enforce endpoint auto-updates via policy (OS + browsers at minimum) with a 7-day deadline.
Set the monthly patch window for servers and network gear; check vendor advisories for edge devices monthly.
Track and report one number to leadership: median days-to-patch for critical/KEV findings.

products.name: Tenable Vulnerability Management
products.note: Agent + network scanning with VPR prioritization; the reporting stands up to auditors and boards.

products.name: Automox (or NinjaOne)
products.note: Cross-platform patch automation for OS and third-party apps, policy-driven with rings.

alternatives.name: Qualys VMDR
alternatives.comparison: Feature-parity rival to Tenable; often wins on bundled pricing when you add patch and compliance modules.

alternatives.name: Rapid7 InsightVM
alternatives.comparison: Strong middle option, best when paired with Rapid7's SIEM/MDR stack rather than standalone.

best_of_breed.ops_notes: Deploy the agent everywhere plus a network scanner for the unmanaged gear — coverage gaps in VM mirror your EDR gaps from Step 4, so reconcile all three against the inventory. Tie remediation tickets to asset owners defined in Step 1 or the queue becomes a graveyard.
config_steps: Deploy Tenable agents fleet-wide and a Nessus scanner for network/appliance coverage.
Baseline scan, then set SLAs by VPR/KEV: critical 7 days, high 30, medium 90 — and publish them.
Roll out Automox rings (test → broad → servers) covering OS plus the top 20 third-party apps.
Auto-create remediation tickets assigned to system owners; escalate SLA breaches weekly.
Report monthly: SLA compliance %, mean-time-to-remediate, and KEV exposure count trend.

BUILD_STEPS.title: Network Visibility & Perimeter
BUILD_STEPS.what: Making the network observable and opinionated: firewall logs flowing to the SIEM with egress (outbound) filtering, protective DNS so malware can't resolve its command-and-control, network detection at key choke points, and a review of segmentation — what can talk to what.
BUILD_STEPS.why: DNS and firewall logs are the cheapest high-value telemetry in security — nearly every attack phase touches them, from the phishing click to data exfiltration. And when (not if) something gets in, segmentation is the difference between one encrypted laptop and an encrypted company.
day_one: Ask where firewall logs go. 'Nowhere' or 'the firewall keeps 7 days' is one of the most common findings in a new environment.
Read the firewall rulebase looking for any-any rules, 'temporary' exceptions from 2021, and inbound port-forwards nobody owns.
Check if the network is flat — can a laptop reach the server VLAN, printers, and cameras directly? Note it for the segmentation plan.
Verify guest WiFi is actually isolated from corporate, not just a different SSID name.
Check what DNS resolvers endpoints use and whether anything logs those queries — that's your cheapest new telemetry win.

products.name: Existing NGFW + Sentinel syslog connector
products.note: Microsoft doesn't sell your firewall — the play is getting its logs and allow/deny policy into Sentinel.

products.name: Cloudflare Gateway (Zero Trust)
products.note: Block malware/C2 domains at DNS with full query logging back into Sentinel.

products.name: Azure NSG flow logs + Azure Firewall (if in Azure)
products.note: Same discipline for cloud VNets: flow visibility and egress restriction.

alternatives.name: Entra Internet Access
alternatives.comparison: Microsoft's SSE answer if you want the whole stack in one vendor — newer and pricier than Cloudflare's free tier for small orgs.

microsoft_native.ops_notes: Firewall logs are voluminous — send denies and threat events to the analytics tier, route bulk allows to the cheap tier. DNS filtering will block something legitimate in week one; pre-agree the exception process so the fix is a ticket, not a shouting match.
config_steps: Point firewall syslog at the Sentinel forwarder; confirm allow/deny events parse correctly.
Review the rulebase: remove any-any rules, document every inbound port-forward with an owner, default-deny egress from server VLANs.
Deploy Cloudflare Gateway DNS policies to all endpoints via Intune; log queries to Sentinel.
Enable NSG flow logs on production VNets if in Azure.
Draft the segmentation target (users / servers / management / IoT-printers / guest) and schedule the changes.

products.name: Security Onion (Suricata + Zeek)
products.note: IDS alerts and rich Zeek metadata from a tap/mirror on your internet edge — enormous visibility for hardware cost.

products.name: Pi-hole or Technitium + Quad9 upstream
products.note: Internal resolver with query logging, malicious domains blocked upstream by Quad9.

products.name: OPNsense / pfSense
products.note: Full-featured open-source firewall with proper logging when the incumbent can't export syslog.

alternatives.name: Quad9 / Cloudflare 1.1.1.2 directly
alternatives.comparison: Zero-infrastructure protective DNS — you lose per-client query logs but gain the blocking in five minutes.

open_source.ops_notes: Security Onion wants real hardware (16GB+ RAM and a dedicated capture NIC) — a retired server is perfect. Force all outbound DNS through your resolver with a firewall rule, or malware simply uses 8.8.8.8 and skips your logging.
config_steps: Configure a SPAN/mirror of the internet edge to a Security Onion sensor; confirm Suricata alerts and Zeek logs flow.
Deploy the internal resolver (Pi-hole/Technitium → Quad9); point DHCP at it and firewall-block direct outbound 53/853.
Ship firewall, resolver, and Security Onion logs into Wazuh.
Audit the firewall rulebase; implement default-deny egress for server VLANs with explicit allows.
Segment in passes: guest isolation verified → printers/IoT to their own VLAN → server VLAN ACLs.

products.name: Cisco Umbrella (or Zscaler)
products.note: Roaming-client DNS + web filtering with mature policy and logging — follows laptops off-network.

products.name: Corelight (Zeek-based NDR)
products.note: Enterprise Zeek with curated detections; the evidence layer investigations rely on.

alternatives.name: Darktrace / Vectra
alternatives.comparison: ML-led NDR with strong east-west anomaly detection; noisier and pricier — demand a POC on your own traffic before buying.

best_of_breed.ops_notes: NDR only sees what you mirror to it — plan tap/SPAN placement (internet edge first, data-center core second) before the PO. Umbrella policies should start at 'security categories only'; content filtering debates can eat a quarter and aren't a SOC problem.
config_steps: Deploy Umbrella roaming clients fleet-wide; enforce security-category blocking, log everything to the SIEM.
Place NDR sensors at the internet edge and DC core via taps/SPAN; validate Zeek logs land in Splunk.
Onboard firewall logs to the SIEM and complete a full rulebase review with owners for every exception.
Implement default-deny egress from server segments; alert on first-seen outbound destinations from servers.
Commission the segmentation project: users / servers / management / OT-IoT / guest, with ACL enforcement.

BUILD_STEPS.title: Detections, Alerting & Triage
BUILD_STEPS.what: Turning telemetry into a workable queue: a curated set of detection rules mapped to MITRE ATT&CK, tuned so the alert volume is humanly reviewable, a severity matrix (P1–P4) with response expectations, and a case management flow so every alert has an owner and an outcome.
BUILD_STEPS.why: Alert fatigue kills SOCs faster than any attacker — an ignored console is the same as no console. Untuned, everything-on alerting trains people to click 'dismiss'; a small set of high-confidence detections that someone actually investigates catches more real attacks than a thousand noisy rules.
day_one: Ask who receives security alerts today. A shared inbox nobody reads is the honest answer at most companies — write it down.
Check what's already firing: existing consoles' alert histories tell you both the noise level and whether anything was ever investigated.
Identify the top 10 noisiest alert sources; they're your first tuning targets.
Define P1–P4 severity with response expectations (P1: page now; P4: weekly review) — one page, agreed with IT.
Pick your first 10 detections against the Step-2 threat model — e.g., MFA method changed + new device, mass file encryption, impossible travel, new global admin.

products.name: Sentinel analytics rules + Defender XDR incidents
products.note: Defender pre-correlates identity/endpoint/email into incidents; Sentinel adds custom KQL rules on top.

products.name: Defender XDR incident queue + Sentinel automation rules
products.note: Assignment, status, and comments in-portal; automation rules handle enrichment and routing.

alternatives.name: Ticketing integration (Jira/ServiceNow)
alternatives.comparison: Route incidents into the company's existing ticket system if that's where work actually gets tracked — visibility beats purity.

microsoft_native.ops_notes: Enable rules in waves of ten and tune each wave before adding more — Sentinel's 'enable all templates' button is how alert fatigue happens by Friday. Hold a weekly 30-minute tuning review: top noisy rules get fixed, suppressed, or killed.
config_steps: Enable the ~10 highest-confidence analytics rules matched to your threat model; map each to ATT&CK.
Configure automation: P1/P2 incidents page or Teams-alert the on-call; P3/P4 queue for daily review.
Publish the severity matrix and the triage runbook: acknowledge → scope → contain → document.
Run the weekly tuning meeting; track alerts-per-day and false-positive rate as first-class metrics.
Test the pipeline monthly with a benign trigger (e.g., a canary account sign-in) and time detection-to-acknowledgment.

products.name: Wazuh rules + Sigma community rules
products.note: Start from Wazuh's defaults plus converted Sigma rules for your log sources; disable liberally.

products.name: TheHive + Cortex
products.note: Real SOC case tracking with observables and enrichment analyzers, at open-source price.

products.name: Shuffle (SOAR)
products.note: Glue: Wazuh alert → TheHive case → Slack/email notification, without writing a service.

alternatives.name: Alerts → email/Slack only
alternatives.comparison: Workable for week one, but with no case state you can't compute MTTR or prove anything was handled — graduate to TheHive by month two.

open_source.ops_notes: The failure mode of a lean SOC is one person silently drowning — cap active detections at what one person can genuinely triage (start: ≤ 20/day) and expand only as tuning earns headroom. Version-control your rule set; rules are code.
config_steps: Curate an initial rule set: ~10 high-confidence detections mapped to your threat model; suppress the noise floor.
Deploy TheHive; wire Wazuh alerts into cases via Shuffle with severity-based routing.
Publish the severity matrix and a one-page triage runbook per top detection.
Set the weekly tuning ritual and track alerts/day, false-positive %, and median time-to-acknowledge.
Fire a monthly end-to-end test event and verify it arrives as a case with the right severity.

products.name: Splunk ES correlation searches (+ detections-as-code via git)
products.note: Risk-based alerting aggregates weak signals into one strong incident — the modern answer to alert fatigue.

products.name: Tines
products.note: No-code automation for enrichment, containment actions, and case routing.

products.name: PagerDuty
products.note: P1s page a human with escalation; everything else respects working hours.

alternatives.name: MDR provider for 24/7 (Expel, Red Canary)
alternatives.comparison: ~$60k+/yr buys someone watching overnight — usually cheaper and better than hiring a night shift.

best_of_breed.ops_notes: Treat detections as code from day one: git repo, peer review, ATT&CK mapping in each rule's metadata, and a deprecation path for stale rules. Measure the funnel weekly — events → alerts → incidents → true positives — and tune whichever stage is leaking.
config_steps: Stand up the detection repo; deploy the first 15–20 ES correlation searches mapped to your threat model.
Enable risk-based alerting so weak signals aggregate per user/host instead of alerting individually.
Build Tines flows: auto-enrich (whois, VT, asset owner) before a human sees the alert; P1 → PagerDuty.
Publish severity matrix + per-detection runbooks; route all incidents through one case system.
Run purple-team validation quarterly (Atomic Red Team) — every detection either fires or gets fixed.

BUILD_STEPS.title: Incident Response & Resilience
BUILD_STEPS.what: The break-glass layer: a written IR plan (roles, contacts, severity definitions, communication rules), runbooks for the five incidents you'll actually face (phishing, BEC, ransomware, compromised account, lost device), tested immutable backups, a tabletop exercise, and the monthly metrics that prove the program works (MTTD, MTTR, coverage).
BUILD_STEPS.why: The first real incident should not be the first time anyone asks 'who calls the lawyer?' or 'can we actually restore?'. Tested backups are the single control that turns ransomware from an existential event into a bad week — and metrics are how a SOC keeps its budget next year.
day_one: Ask if an IR plan exists and when it was last touched; a 2019 PDF with departed employees' names counts as 'no'.
Read the cyber insurance policy — it often mandates specific IR firms, notification windows, and controls; violating it can void coverage.
Verify backup coverage against the crown jewels from Step 2, and find the date of the last successful *restore test* — not the last backup.
Check whether backups are isolated/immutable; ransomware crews delete reachable backups first, before encrypting anything.
Establish who can take emergency action at 2am — isolate a host, disable an account, block a domain — and how to reach them.

products.name: Microsoft 365 Backup (or a third-party like Veeam M365)
products.note: Retention policies are not backups — you need restorable copies of Exchange/SharePoint/OneDrive.

products.name: Azure Backup with immutable vaults
products.note: Immutability + soft delete means a compromised admin can't purge your recovery point.

products.name: NIST 800-61 + CISA playbooks; Microsoft IR retainer optional
products.note: Don't write the plan from scratch — adapt the free frameworks to your org chart.

alternatives.name: Commercial IR retainer (CrowdStrike, Mandiant)
alternatives.comparison: Pre-negotiated hourly rates and a hotline; often satisfies the insurance requirement and beats shopping for help mid-breach.

microsoft_native.ops_notes: Schedule the restore test like patching — quarterly, calendared, with the result reported. The tabletop in month 3 will surface the real gaps (nobody knows who talks to customers); that's the point, run it anyway.
config_steps: Write the IR plan from NIST 800-61: roles, severity definitions, out-of-band comms channel, legal/insurance contacts.
Write the five runbooks: phishing, BEC, ransomware, compromised account, lost/stolen device.
Deploy M365 + Azure backups with immutability; verify coverage against the crown-jewel list.
Run a restore test now and quarterly; record time-to-restore for a representative workload.
Run a ransomware tabletop with leadership in month 3; publish the findings and fix the top three.

products.name: Veeam Community Edition (≤10 workloads) / restic + object storage
products.note: 3-2-1 with an offline or object-lock (immutable) copy; restic to Backblaze B2 or S3 with object-lock is remarkably cheap.

products.name: NIST 800-61 + CISA ransomware guide + SANS templates
products.note: Adapt, don't author — fill in your names, numbers, and systems.

products.name: TheHive (from Step 9)
products.note: Incidents are cases with a timeline; the same tool carries you through post-incident review.

alternatives.name: Proxmox Backup Server / BorgBackup
alternatives.comparison: Excellent free alternatives depending on your virtualization stack — the immutability/off-site property matters more than the tool.

open_source.ops_notes: On a lean budget the discipline substitutes for the tooling: quarterly restore test on the calendar, the IR contact sheet printed (yes, paper — the wiki may be encrypted too), and one tabletop a year minimum. Know who you'd call for a real breach before you need them.
config_steps: Implement 3-2-1 backups for crown jewels with one immutable/offline copy; document restore procedures.
Run a full restore test; time it and file the result. Repeat quarterly.
Adapt NIST/CISA templates into a short IR plan + five runbooks; print the contact sheet.
Define emergency authority: who may isolate/disable/block without approval, at any hour.
Run a tabletop (CISA's free exercise packages work); track MTTD/MTTR from TheHive monthly.

products.name: Veeam (licensed) or Rubrik
products.note: Immutable, air-gap-capable backup with fast granular restore — the ransomware kill-switch, funded properly.

products.name: CrowdStrike Services / Mandiant retainer
products.note: A hotline with pre-negotiated terms and your environment pre-documented; insurance often expects it.

products.name: Annual third-party tabletop
products.note: An outside facilitator makes executives take the exercise seriously — that's most of the value.

alternatives.name: Breach & attack simulation (SafeBreach, AttackIQ)
alternatives.comparison: Continuous control validation — a year-two maturity step once the foundations here are solid.

best_of_breed.ops_notes: Make the metrics reviewable by non-engineers: MTTD, MTTR, EDR/backup/MFA coverage %, phishing report rate, patch SLA compliance — one page, monthly, trend arrows. That page is how the program defends its budget.
config_steps: Deploy the backup platform with immutability; validate crown-jewel coverage and run the first restore test.
Sign the IR retainer; do the pre-engagement environment documentation while nothing is on fire.
Finalize the IR plan + five runbooks; integrate paging (Step 9) with the escalation tree.
Run the facilitated tabletop in month 3–4 with executives present; publish and fix the top findings.
Stand up the monthly one-page SOC scorecard (MTTD, MTTR, coverage %, phish report rate, patch SLA %).

SOURCES.note: Defender XDR, Sentinel, Entra, Intune licensing and docs
